articles.headline: Manipur CM N. Biren Singh Resigns Amidst Ongoing Ethnic Violence
articles.explainer:
• Chief Minister N. Biren Singh submitted his resignation to the Governor, citing the inability to control the escalating ethnic violence.
• The resignation comes after months of unrest between the Meitei and Kuki communities, leading to significant loss of life and displacement.
• Political analysts suggest the move could pave the way for President's Rule or a new coalition government to restore stability.
• The central government had previously deployed additional paramilitary forces and appointed a security advisor to manage the crisis.
• This development highlights the severe governance challenges and internal security threats facing the state.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution - historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions
Role of civil services in a democracy
Internal Security challenges
prelimsPoints: N. Biren Singh was the Chief Minister of Manipur.
Manipur is located in Northeast India, sharing borders with Myanmar.
The conflict is primarily between the Meitei (majority in Imphal Valley) and Kuki (majority in hill districts) communities.
Article 356 of the Indian Constitution pertains to President's Rule in states.
mainsPoints: Analyze the socio-economic and political factors contributing to ethnic violence in Manipur.
Discuss the role and limitations of state and central governments in managing prolonged internal conflicts.
Evaluate the implications of a Chief Minister's resignation on state governance and federal relations.
Suggest comprehensive measures for conflict resolution and long-term peacebuilding in ethnically diverse regions.
articles.imageDescription: Map of Manipur showing Meitei and Kuki dominated areas.
articles.trivia: Manipur, meaning 'A jeweled land', is known for its classical dance form, Manipuri, and is considered the birthplace of modern polo.

articles.headline: Major Anti-Naxal Operation in Bijapur: 31 Naxalites, 2 Security Personnel Killed
articles.explainer:
• A fierce encounter in Chhattisgarh's Bijapur district resulted in the death of 31 Naxalites and two security personnel.
• The joint operation involved forces from the District Reserve Guard (DRG), Special Task Force (STF), and CoBRA unit.
• This marks one of the deadliest clashes in recent years against Left-Wing Extremism (LWE) in the Bastar region.
• Security forces recovered a large cache of arms, ammunition, and Naxalite literature from the encounter site.
• The incident underscores the persistent challenge of LWE in central India and the ongoing efforts to neutralize the threat.
gsSubTopics: Linkages between development and spread of extremism
Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security

Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: Bijapur district is part of the Bastar region in Chhattisgarh, a known LWE stronghold.
DRG (District Reserve Guard), STF (Special Task Force), and CoBRA (Commando Battalion for Resolute Action) are specialized anti-Naxal units.
Left-Wing Extremism is primarily concentrated in the 'Red Corridor' region of India.
The term 'Naxalite' originated from Naxalbari village in West Bengal.
mainsPoints: Analyze the effectiveness of current government strategies in combating Left-Wing Extremism.
Discuss the socio-economic factors that contribute to the persistence of Naxalism in regions like Bastar.
Evaluate the challenges faced by security forces in anti-Naxal operations and suggest measures for improvement.
Examine the balance between security operations and developmental initiatives in addressing LWE.
articles.imageDescription: Security personnel conducting an operation in a forest area.
articles.trivia: The 'Red Corridor' is a region in eastern, central, and southern India that experiences considerable Naxalite-Maoist insurgency.

articles.headline: IAF Mirage 2000 Fighter Jet Crashes in MP, Pilots Eject Safely
articles.explainer:
• A Dassault Mirage 2000 fighter aircraft of the Indian Air Force (IAF) crashed during a routine training sortie in Shivpuri district, Madhya Pradesh.
• Both pilots on board managed to eject safely and were subsequently rescued with minor injuries.
• An inquiry has been ordered by the IAF to ascertain the cause of the accident.
• The Mirage 2000 is a multirole, single-engine fourth-generation fighter jet, a significant component of the IAF's fleet.
• The incident highlights the inherent risks associated with military aviation and the importance of stringent safety protocols and maintenance.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: Mirage 2000 is a French-made multirole fighter jet, manufactured by Dassault Aviation.
Shivpuri district is located in the Gwalior division of Madhya Pradesh.
The Indian Air Force is one of the three branches of the Indian Armed Forces.
Ejection seats are safety devices designed to rescue pilots in emergencies.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges in maintaining an aging fleet of fighter aircraft and the need for modernization in the IAF.
Examine the role of indigenous defence production in enhancing India's aerospace safety and self-reliance.
Analyze the factors contributing to military aircraft accidents and suggest measures for improving aviation safety.
Evaluate the importance of continuous training and advanced technology for pilot safety in high-performance aircraft.
articles.imageDescription: A Dassault Mirage 2000 fighter jet in flight.
articles.trivia: The Mirage 2000 was first inducted into the Indian Air Force in 1985 and played a crucial role in the Kargil War.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Delivers Landmark Verdict on Electoral Bonds Scheme
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a significant ruling, declared the Electoral Bonds Scheme unconstitutional, citing violations of citizens' right to information and transparency in political funding.
• The court directed the State Bank of India (SBI) to immediately cease issuing electoral bonds and to disclose all details of bonds purchased since 2019 to the Election Commission of India (ECI).
• The verdict emphasized that anonymous political donations undermine the principles of free and fair elections and democratic accountability.
• This decision is expected to have far-reaching implications for political parties and their funding mechanisms.
• The ECI has been tasked with publishing the disclosed information on its official website within a stipulated timeframe.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution - historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions
Parliament and State Legislatures – structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
prelimsPoints: The Electoral Bonds Scheme was introduced in 2017.
Article 19(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution guarantees the right to freedom of speech and expression, which includes the right to information.
The Election Commission of India (ECI) is a constitutional body responsible for conducting elections.
State Bank of India (SBI) was the only authorized bank to issue electoral bonds.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional implications of the Supreme Court's verdict on the Electoral Bonds Scheme.
Discuss the arguments for and against electoral bonds in promoting transparency and accountability in political funding.
Evaluate the potential impact of this judgment on political parties' fundraising strategies and electoral reforms in India.
Suggest alternative mechanisms for transparent political funding that uphold democratic principles.
articles.trivia: The first electoral bonds were issued in March 2018.

articles.headline: RBI Holds Repo Rate Steady, Cites Inflation Concerns in MPC Meet
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) unanimously decided to maintain the repo rate at its current level for the sixth consecutive time.
• RBI Governor Shaktikanta Das highlighted persistent concerns over inflation, especially food inflation, as the primary reason for the pause.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target while supporting economic growth.
• The central bank revised its inflation projection marginally upwards for the next quarter, signaling a cautious outlook.
• Analysts anticipate that the RBI will continue to adopt a 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance, keeping liquidity tight.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.

Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System- objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
Food processing and related industries in India- scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management.
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) consists of six members (three from RBI, three external).
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
RBI's inflation target is 4% with a +/- 2% band.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors influencing the RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate amidst varying economic indicators.
Discuss the impact of a sustained 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance on different sectors of the Indian economy.
Evaluate the challenges posed by food inflation to India's overall inflation targeting framework.
Examine the role of the RBI's monetary policy in achieving macroeconomic stability and fostering sustainable growth.
articles.imageDescription: Reserve Bank of India building in Mumbai.
articles.trivia: The first Monetary Policy Committee meeting was held in October 2016.

articles.headline: India, EU Announce Significant Progress in Free Trade Agreement Negotiations
articles.explainer:
• India and the European Union (EU) concluded the latest round of Free Trade Agreement (FTA) negotiations, announcing significant progress on several key chapters.
• Both sides expressed optimism about reaching a comprehensive agreement, potentially by year-end, which could boost bilateral trade and investment.
• Discussions focused on market access for goods and services, intellectual property rights, and sustainable development provisions.
• The FTA aims to reduce tariffs, eliminate non-tariff barriers, and create a more predictable and transparent trade environment.
• A successful deal would be crucial for India's ambition to expand its global trade footprint and for the EU to diversify its supply chains.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora- their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: The European Union is an economic and political union of 27 member states.
An FTA aims to liberalize trade by reducing or eliminating tariffs and other trade barriers.
India is negotiating FTAs with several key partners, including the UK, Australia, and Canada.
The Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT) is responsible for implementing India's foreign trade policy.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic and economic significance of an India-EU Free Trade Agreement for both parties.
Discuss the potential challenges and opportunities for Indian industries arising from a comprehensive FTA with the EU.
Evaluate the role of FTAs in enhancing India's global trade competitiveness and achieving its economic goals.
Examine how sustainable development and labor standards are integrated into modern trade agreements.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and the European Union.
articles.trivia: The EU is India's third-largest trading partner, after the US and China.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite EOS-08
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched the Earth Observation Satellite (EOS-08) aboard the Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV-C58) from Sriharikota.
• EOS-08 is designed to provide high-resolution imagery for applications in agriculture, forestry, disaster management, and urban planning.
• This mission further strengthens India's remote sensing capabilities and its commitment to utilizing space technology for national development.
• The PSLV-C58 also carried several co-passenger satellites, including some from private Indian companies and international clients, promoting space collaboration.
• The launch demonstrates ISRO's continued prowess in deploying advanced satellites and its role in fostering India's growing space economy.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation) is India's national space agency.
PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) is a third-generation launch vehicle.
Earth observation satellites are used for monitoring Earth's surface, atmosphere, and oceans.
Sriharikota is the location of the Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC), India's primary spaceport.
mainsPoints: Discuss the applications of earth observation satellites in various sectors of the Indian economy and governance.
Evaluate India's achievements in space technology and its contributions to global space exploration.
Examine the role of ISRO in promoting private sector participation and fostering a vibrant space ecosystem in India.
Analyze the strategic importance of advanced remote sensing capabilities for national security and disaster management.
articles.imageDescription: PSLV rocket lifting off from the launch pad.
articles.trivia: PSLV holds the record for launching the most satellites in a single mission (104 satellites in 2017).

articles.headline: Government Unveils National AI Strategy 2.0: Focus on Ethical AI & Skilling
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched the National AI Strategy 2.0, building on the previous 'AI for All' vision.
• The updated strategy emphasizes responsible and ethical development of Artificial Intelligence, with a focus on data privacy and algorithmic transparency.
• Key pillars include fostering AI innovation in critical sectors like healthcare and agriculture, promoting AI skilling, and establishing a robust governance framework.
• The strategy also outlines plans for setting up dedicated AI research centers and encouraging public-private partnerships in AI development.
• India aims to position itself as a global leader in ethical and inclusive AI, leveraging its demographic dividend and technological prowess.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: MeitY (Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology) is the nodal ministry for AI policy in India.
The first National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence was released in 2018 by NITI Aayog.
Ethical AI concerns include bias, privacy, and accountability.
India is a member of the Global Partnership on Artificial Intelligence (GPAI).
mainsPoints: Discuss the key objectives and components of India's National AI Strategy 2.0 and its potential impact on various sectors.
Analyze the ethical challenges associated with the rapid development and deployment of Artificial Intelligence.
Evaluate India's potential to become a global hub for ethical and inclusive AI, considering its strengths and weaknesses.
Examine the role of government policy in fostering AI innovation while addressing concerns related to job displacement and data security.
articles.trivia: India is one of the founding members of the Global Partnership on Artificial Intelligence (GPAI).

articles.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Stricter Enforcement of Digital Personal Data Protection Act
articles.explainer:
• A Parliamentary Standing Committee on Information Technology submitted its report, recommending stricter enforcement mechanisms for the recently enacted Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
• The committee highlighted concerns regarding the adequacy of the Data Protection Board's powers and resources to handle potential violations effectively.
• It suggested enhancing penalties for data breaches and ensuring swift grievance redressal for citizens.
• The report also called for greater public awareness campaigns about data protection rights and responsibilities.
• The recommendations aim to strengthen India's data governance framework and build trust in the digital economy.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
Role of civil services in a democracy.
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, aims to regulate the processing of digital personal data.
The Data Protection Board of India is established under this Act.
A Parliamentary Standing Committee is a permanent committee of Parliament.
The right to privacy was declared a fundamental right by the Supreme Court in the Puttaswamy judgment (2017).
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, and its significance for individual privacy.
Discuss the challenges in enforcing data protection laws in a rapidly evolving digital landscape.
Evaluate the recommendations of the Parliamentary Committee in strengthening the data governance framework.
Examine the balance between data innovation and individual privacy rights in India's digital economy.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized image of data security and privacy.
articles.trivia: India's first attempt at a comprehensive data protection law began with the Justice Srikrishna Committee report in 2018.

articles.headline: Himachal Pradesh Grapples with Rapid Glacier Melt, Threatening Water Security
articles.explainer:
• A new report by the Indian Institute of Science (IISc) highlights an alarming rate of glacier melt in Himachal Pradesh, posing a severe threat to the state's water security.
• The study indicates that several glaciers in the Western Himalayas are receding faster than global averages, impacting downstream rivers like the Chenab, Ravi, and Beas.
• This rapid melt could lead to increased flash floods and GLOFs (Glacial Lake Outburst Floods) in the short term, followed by water scarcity in the long term.
• The findings underscore the urgent need for climate change adaptation strategies and sustainable water resource management in the Himalayan region.
• Local communities, heavily reliant on glacier-fed rivers for agriculture and hydropower, are particularly vulnerable.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of world’s physical geography.

Distribution of key natural resources across the world (including South Asia and the Indian subcontinent); factors responsible for the location of primary, secondary, and tertiary sector industries in various parts of the world (including India).

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location- changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
Environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: Himachal Pradesh is a state in the Western Himalayas.
Major rivers originating from Himachal Pradesh include Chenab, Ravi, Beas, Sutlej, and Yamuna.
GLOFs are sudden releases of water from glacial lakes due to dam failure.
The Himalayas are often referred to as the 'Third Pole' due to their vast ice reserves.
mainsPoints: Analyze the socio-economic and environmental impacts of rapid glacier melt in the Himalayan region, particularly on states like Himachal Pradesh.
Discuss the current strategies and policy gaps in India's approach to climate change adaptation in mountainous ecosystems.
Evaluate the challenges in ensuring long-term water security for downstream populations dependent on Himalayan rivers.
Suggest integrated approaches involving scientific research, policy interventions, and community participation to address glacier recession.
articles.imageDescription: Melting glacier in the Himalayas with a river flowing from it.
articles.trivia: Around 12% of India's landmass is covered by the Himalayas, which contain the largest volume of ice outside the polar regions.

articles.headline: New 'PM-Awas Grameen' Plus Scheme to Address Rural Housing Deficit
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved the launch of the 'Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana – Gramin (PMAY-G) Plus' scheme, a revamped version of the existing rural housing program.
• The 'Plus' scheme aims to achieve the 'Housing for All' target by 2027, focusing on providing pucca houses with basic amenities to eligible rural households.
• Enhanced financial assistance, convergence with other government schemes (like Swachh Bharat Mission for toilets), and use of innovative construction technologies are key features.
• Special emphasis will be placed on remote areas, tribal communities, and vulnerable groups to ensure inclusive development.
• The scheme is expected to generate significant employment in rural areas and improve the living standards of millions.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: PMAY-G was launched in 2016.
The scheme aims to provide a pucca house with basic amenities.
It operates under the Ministry of Rural Development.
Convergence with other schemes like MGNREGA (for labor) and Swachh Bharat Mission (for toilets) is a key feature.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and key features of the PMAY-G Plus scheme in addressing the rural housing deficit.
Discuss the challenges in implementing large-scale housing schemes in rural India, including land availability and material costs.
Evaluate the role of inter-scheme convergence in enhancing the effectiveness and impact of welfare programs.
Examine how improved housing contributes to socio-economic development and human dignity in rural communities.
articles.imageDescription: A newly constructed rural house under a government scheme.
articles.trivia: Before PMAY-G, the major rural housing program was the Indira Awaas Yojana (IAY).

articles.headline: India Kicks Off Preparations for 5th India-Africa Forum Summit (IAFS)
articles.explainer:
• India has initiated preparatory meetings for the 5th edition of the India-Africa Forum Summit (IAFS), expected to be held later this year.
• The summit aims to strengthen India's strategic partnership with African nations across various sectors, including trade, investment, capacity building, and defense.
• Discussions focused on setting the agenda, identifying key themes such as climate change, digital transformation, and health security, and ensuring wide participation.
• IAFS is India's premier mechanism for engaging with the entire African continent at a political level.
• This renewed engagement underscores India's commitment to South-South cooperation and its growing geopolitical significance in the Indo-Pacific.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora- their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: The first IAFS was held in 2008.
IAFS is a major platform for India's engagement with African countries.
India's 'Act East' policy has a strong economic and strategic dimension.
Africa is a continent of 54 recognized countries.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolution and significance of India's engagement with Africa, particularly through the IAFS framework.
Discuss the key areas of cooperation between India and African nations and the mutual benefits derived from this partnership.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India in strengthening its strategic and economic ties with the African continent.
Examine how India's approach to Africa differs from that of other major global powers and its implications for South-South cooperation.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and various African nations.
articles.trivia: The 3rd IAFS, held in 2015, saw participation from all 54 African countries, making it the largest gathering of African leaders in India.

articles.headline: Ministry of Health Launches Nationwide Campaign for NCD Prevention
articles.explainer:
• The Union Ministry of Health and Family Welfare launched a comprehensive nationwide campaign aimed at preventing and controlling Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs).
• The campaign focuses on promoting healthy lifestyles, early detection, and management of NCDs such as diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular diseases, and cancers.
• Initiatives include widespread awareness programs, free screening camps, and improved access to diagnostics and treatment facilities at primary healthcare centers.
• Emphasis is also placed on reducing risk factors like tobacco use, unhealthy diets, physical inactivity, and harmful alcohol consumption.
• The move is crucial as NCDs account for a significant portion of mortality and morbidity in India, posing a major public health challenge.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: NCDs are diseases that are not transmitted from person to person.
Examples of NCDs include diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular diseases, and various cancers.
Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY) is a flagship health scheme.
The National Programme for Prevention & Control of Cancer, Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases & Stroke (NPCDCS) is a key initiative.
mainsPoints: Analyze the growing burden of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) in India and their socio-economic implications.
Discuss the multi-faceted approach required for effective NCD prevention and control, encompassing lifestyle changes, screening, and treatment.
Evaluate the role of government initiatives and public-private partnerships in strengthening India's public healthcare system to tackle NCDs.
Examine the challenges in promoting healthy behaviors and ensuring equitable access to NCD care across diverse populations.
articles.imageDescription: People engaging in healthy activities like yoga and eating fruits.
articles.trivia: Globally, NCDs kill 41 million people each year, equivalent to 74% of all deaths.

articles.headline: Defence Ministry Greenlights Indigenous Submarine Development Project
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Defence announced the approval of a mega 'Make in India' project for the indigenous design and construction of advanced conventional submarines.
• This strategic move aims to bolster India's naval capabilities and achieve greater self-reliance in critical defence technologies.
• The project, under 'Strategic Partnership' model, will involve collaboration between an Indian private sector shipyard and a foreign original equipment manufacturer (OEM).
• The new submarines are expected to feature advanced stealth, combat management systems, and long-range targeting capabilities.
• This initiative is a significant step towards fulfilling the Indian Navy's long-term modernization plans and strengthening maritime security.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: The 'Make in India' initiative promotes domestic manufacturing.
The 'Strategic Partnership' model aims to create a robust defence industrial base.
Conventional submarines are diesel-electric submarines, unlike nuclear submarines.
The Indian Navy is one of the three branches of the Indian Armed Forces.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic rationale behind India's push for indigenous defence production, particularly in critical areas like submarine development.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities associated with the 'Strategic Partnership' model in fostering self-reliance in defence manufacturing.
Evaluate the importance of a strong naval force, including advanced submarines, for India's maritime security and geopolitical interests.
Examine the technological advancements and skill development required to achieve sustained indigenous capability in complex defence systems.
articles.imageDescription: A silhouette of a submarine at sea.
articles.trivia: Project 75 (I) is a previous Indian Navy project for the acquisition of six conventional submarines.

articles.headline: Report Highlights Impact of Climate Change on Indian Agriculture
articles.explainer:
• A comprehensive report by the Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) detailed the severe and varied impacts of climate change on Indian agriculture.
• The report points to increased frequency of extreme weather events, such as droughts, floods, and unseasonal rains, leading to significant crop losses.
• It also highlights shifts in cropping patterns, reduced yield potential of major crops, and increased vulnerability to pests and diseases due to changing climatic conditions.
• The findings emphasize the urgent need for climate-resilient agriculture practices, improved irrigation systems, and development of stress-tolerant crop varieties.
• Food security and farmers' livelihoods are at significant risk, necessitating immediate policy interventions and technological advancements.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.

Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System- objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: ICAR (Indian Council of Agricultural Research) is an autonomous organization under the Department of Agricultural Research and Education (DARE), Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare.
India is highly vulnerable to climate change due to its large agricultural sector and monsoon dependence.
M.S. Swaminathan is known as the 'Father of the Green Revolution' in India.
Droughts, floods, and heatwaves are common extreme weather events in India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multifaceted impacts of climate change on Indian agriculture and its implications for food security and rural livelihoods.
Discuss the various strategies and technologies available for building climate resilience in the agricultural sector.
Evaluate the role of government policies, research institutions, and farmer-centric initiatives in adapting to climate change.
Examine the challenges in implementing climate-resilient agriculture practices across diverse agro-climatic zones in India.
articles.imageDescription: A field of crops affected by drought.
articles.trivia: Agriculture contributes around 18-20% of India's GDP and employs over 50% of the workforce.

articles.headline: Initiative to Revitalize Traditional Indian Crafts and Handlooms Launched
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Textiles, in collaboration with various state governments, launched a new national initiative aimed at revitalizing traditional Indian crafts and handlooms.
• The program focuses on skill development, design innovation, market linkages, and digital promotion for artisans and weavers.
• It seeks to preserve India's rich cultural heritage while enhancing the economic viability of traditional craft sectors.
• Financial assistance, access to raw materials, and intellectual property protection for unique craft forms are key components.
• The initiative is expected to empower millions of artisans, particularly women, and boost the 'Vocal for Local' campaign.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
Role of women and women’s organization, population and associated issues, poverty and developmental issues, urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
Effects of globalization on Indian society.
prelimsPoints: India is known for its diverse range of traditional crafts and handlooms, such as Pashmina, Kanjeevaram, Banarasi, etc.
The Ministry of Textiles is responsible for the growth and development of the textile industry.
'Vocal for Local' is an initiative to promote local products.
GI (Geographical Indication) tags are used to protect unique products from specific regions.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of traditional Indian crafts and handlooms in preserving cultural heritage and promoting economic empowerment.
Discuss the challenges faced by artisans and weavers in the modern market and the need for policy interventions.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government initiatives like 'Vocal for Local' and skill development programs in revitalizing the sector.
Examine how digital platforms and design innovation can help traditional crafts reach a wider global audience.
articles.imageDescription: An artisan weaving on a handloom.
articles.trivia: India is one of the largest producers of handloom fabrics in the world.

highlights: Manipur CM N. Biren Singh resigns amidst escalating ethnic violence, raising concerns about state stability.
Major anti-Naxal operation in Bijapur, Chhattisgarh, results in 33 casualties including 31 Naxalites and 2 security personnel.
Supreme Court declares Electoral Bonds Scheme unconstitutional, mandating SBI to disclose all donation details to ECI.
RBI's Monetary Policy Committee keeps repo rate unchanged, citing persistent inflation concerns.
ISRO successfully launches advanced Earth Observation Satellite EOS-08, boosting India's remote sensing capabilities.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the recent developments in Manipur, consider the following statements:
1. N. Biren Singh has resigned as the Chief Minister of Manipur.
2. The ethnic violence in Manipur is primarily between the Meitei and Naga communities.
3. The Governor's role becomes crucial in forming a new government or recommending President's Rule under such circumstances.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct as N. Biren Singh resigned as CM. Statement 2 is incorrect; the primary ethnic violence in Manipur has been between the Meitei and Kuki communities. Statement 3 is correct, as the Governor plays a key constitutional role in such political crises, including the possibility of President's Rule (Article 356).

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding Left-Wing Extremism (LWE) in India:
1. Bijapur district, a site of recent clashes, is located in the Bastar region of Chhattisgarh.
2. CoBRA is a specialized unit of the Border Security Force (BSF) deployed for anti-Naxal operations.
3. The term 'Naxalite' originated from a village in Andhra Pradesh.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. Bijapur is indeed in the Bastar region of Chhattisgarh. Statement 2 is incorrect; CoBRA (Commando Battalion for Resolute Action) is a specialized unit of the Central Reserve Police Force (CRPF), not BSF. Statement 3 is incorrect; the term 'Naxalite' originated from Naxalbari village in West Bengal.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about the 'Mirage 2000' fighter jet is/are correct?
1. It is a multirole, single-engine fourth-generation fighter jet.
2. It is manufactured by the American company Lockheed Martin.
3. It played a significant role in the Kargil War.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The Mirage 2000 is a multirole, single-engine fourth-generation fighter jet. Statement 2 is incorrect. It is manufactured by the French company Dassault Aviation, not Lockheed Martin. Statement 3 is correct. The Mirage 2000 played a crucial role for the Indian Air Force during the Kargil War.

prelimsMocks.question:
Regarding the Electoral Bonds Scheme, which was recently declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court, consider the following:
1. The scheme was introduced in 2017.
2. The State Bank of India (SBI) was the only authorized bank to issue electoral bonds.
3. The Supreme Court cited violation of the Right to Information as a key reason for its unconstitutionality.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. The Electoral Bonds Scheme was introduced in 2017. SBI was indeed the only authorized bank. The Supreme Court's ruling highlighted that anonymous political donations violated citizens' right to information and transparency.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy, which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) comprises six members, all from the RBI.
2. The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
3. A reduction in the repo rate typically leads to an increase in liquidity in the banking system.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect. The MPC comprises six members: three from the RBI and three external members appointed by the government. Statement 2 is correct. This is the mandated objective. Statement 3 is correct. A lower repo rate encourages banks to borrow more from the RBI, injecting liquidity into the system.

mainsMocks.question: The resignation of the Manipur Chief Minister underscores the complex interplay of ethnic grievances, governance challenges, and internal security threats. Critically analyze the root causes of the prolonged ethnic violence in Manipur and suggest comprehensive policy measures to restore peace and foster inclusive development.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure; GS3: Linkages between development and spread of extremism; Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the context of CM's resignation and Manipur violence. Discuss historical, socio-economic, political, and administrative root causes (e.g., land, resources, identity, governance deficit, drug trade). Propose policy measures including dialogue, security reforms, development initiatives, rehabilitation, and strengthening local governance.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's verdict on electoral bonds is a landmark decision for transparency in political funding. Discuss how the Electoral Bonds Scheme impacted democratic accountability and freedom of information. In light of the judgment, what alternative mechanisms can be explored to ensure transparent and equitable political financing in India?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Indian Constitution - significant provisions; Parliament and State Legislatures – structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these; Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
mainsMocks.approach: Begin with the SC verdict and its significance. Explain the Electoral Bonds Scheme and its stated objectives. Analyze how it undermined transparency, Right to Information, and democratic accountability. Suggest alternatives like direct state funding, electoral trusts, digital platforms for donations, stricter disclosure norms, and strengthening ECI's powers.

mainsMocks.question: India's ambition to become a global leader in Artificial Intelligence (AI) necessitates a robust and ethical framework. Critically examine the key objectives of India's National AI Strategy 2.0. What are the ethical challenges associated with AI deployment, and how can India balance innovation with responsible AI governance?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life; Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce India's AI ambitions and the Strategy 2.0. Detail its key objectives (e.g., AI for all, innovation, skilling, governance, ethical AI). Discuss ethical challenges (e.g., bias, privacy, accountability, job displacement, surveillance). Propose measures for responsible AI governance, including regulatory frameworks, public awareness, international collaboration, and ethical guidelines.

quickBytes.text: The Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC) is located in Sriharikota, Andhra Pradesh, and is ISRO's primary spaceport.
The 'Red Corridor' is an area in central and eastern India affected by Left-Wing Extremism (LWE).
Article 356 of the Indian Constitution empowers the President to impose 'President's Rule' in a state.
The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is statutorily mandated to meet at least four times in a year.
GLOFs (Glacial Lake Outburst Floods) are sudden releases of water from glacial lakes, a hazard in the Himalayas.
The Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT) implements India's foreign trade policy.
The 'Strategic Partnership' model in defence aims to build capabilities in private sector shipyards and industries.
The right to privacy was declared a fundamental right under Article 21 by the Supreme Court in 2017.
Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) are also known as chronic diseases and are not transmitted from person to person.
The 'Vocal for Local' campaign promotes indigenous products and supports local businesses and artisans.

GS1.headline: Himachal Pradesh Grapples with Rapid Glacier Melt, Threatening Water Security
GS1.explainer:
• A new report by the Indian Institute of Science (IISc) highlights an alarming rate of glacier melt in Himachal Pradesh, posing a severe threat to the state's water security.
• The study indicates that several glaciers in the Western Himalayas are receding faster than global averages, impacting downstream rivers like the Chenab, Ravi, and Beas.
• This rapid melt could lead to increased flash floods and GLOFs (Glacial Lake Outburst Floods) in the short term, followed by water scarcity in the long term.
• The findings underscore the urgent need for climate change adaptation strategies and sustainable water resource management in the Himalayan region.
• Local communities, heavily reliant on glacier-fed rivers for agriculture and hydropower, are particularly vulnerable.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of world’s physical geography.

Distribution of key natural resources across the world (including South Asia and the Indian subcontinent); factors responsible for the location of primary, secondary, and tertiary sector industries in various parts of the world (including India).

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location- changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
Environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: Himachal Pradesh is a state in the Western Himalayas.
Major rivers originating from Himachal Pradesh include Chenab, Ravi, Beas, Sutlej, and Yamuna.
GLOFs are sudden releases of water from glacial lakes due to dam failure.
The Himalayas are often referred to as the 'Third Pole' due to their vast ice reserves.
mainsPoints: Analyze the socio-economic and environmental impacts of rapid glacier melt in the Himalayan region, particularly on states like Himachal Pradesh.
Discuss the current strategies and policy gaps in India's approach to climate change adaptation in mountainous ecosystems.
Evaluate the challenges in ensuring long-term water security for downstream populations dependent on Himalayan rivers.
Suggest integrated approaches involving scientific research, policy interventions, and community participation to address glacier recession.
GS1.imageDescription: Melting glacier in the Himalayas with a river flowing from it.
GS1.trivia: Around 12% of India's landmass is covered by the Himalayas, which contain the largest volume of ice outside the polar regions.

GS1.headline: Initiative to Revitalize Traditional Indian Crafts and Handlooms Launched
GS1.explainer:
• The Ministry of Textiles, in collaboration with various state governments, launched a new national initiative aimed at revitalizing traditional Indian crafts and handlooms.
• The program focuses on skill development, design innovation, market linkages, and digital promotion for artisans and weavers.
• It seeks to preserve India's rich cultural heritage while enhancing the economic viability of traditional craft sectors.
• Financial assistance, access to raw materials, and intellectual property protection for unique craft forms are key components.
• The initiative is expected to empower millions of artisans, particularly women, and boost the 'Vocal for Local' campaign.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
Role of women and women’s organization, population and associated issues, poverty and developmental issues, urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
Effects of globalization on Indian society.
prelimsPoints: India is known for its diverse range of traditional crafts and handlooms, such as Pashmina, Kanjeevaram, Banarasi, etc.
The Ministry of Textiles is responsible for the growth and development of the textile industry.
'Vocal for Local' is an initiative to promote local products.
GI (Geographical Indication) tags are used to protect unique products from specific regions.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of traditional Indian crafts and handlooms in preserving cultural heritage and promoting economic empowerment.
Discuss the challenges faced by artisans and weavers in the modern market and the need for policy interventions.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government initiatives like 'Vocal for Local' and skill development programs in revitalizing the sector.
Examine how digital platforms and design innovation can help traditional crafts reach a wider global audience.
GS1.imageDescription: An artisan weaving on a handloom.
GS1.trivia: India is one of the largest producers of handloom fabrics in the world.

GS2.headline: Manipur CM N. Biren Singh Resigns Amidst Ongoing Ethnic Violence
GS2.explainer:
• Chief Minister N. Biren Singh submitted his resignation to the Governor, citing the inability to control the escalating ethnic violence.
• The resignation comes after months of unrest between the Meitei and Kuki communities, leading to significant loss of life and displacement.
• Political analysts suggest the move could pave the way for President's Rule or a new coalition government to restore stability.
• The central government had previously deployed additional paramilitary forces and appointed a security advisor to manage the crisis.
• This development highlights the severe governance challenges and internal security threats facing the state.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution - historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions
Role of civil services in a democracy
Internal Security challenges
prelimsPoints: N. Biren Singh was the Chief Minister of Manipur.
Manipur is located in Northeast India, sharing borders with Myanmar.
The conflict is primarily between the Meitei (majority in Imphal Valley) and Kuki (majority in hill districts) communities.
Article 356 of the Indian Constitution pertains to President's Rule in states.
mainsPoints: Analyze the socio-economic and political factors contributing to ethnic violence in Manipur.
Discuss the role and limitations of state and central governments in managing prolonged internal conflicts.
Evaluate the implications of a Chief Minister's resignation on state governance and federal relations.
Suggest comprehensive measures for conflict resolution and long-term peacebuilding in ethnically diverse regions.
GS2.imageDescription: Map of Manipur showing Meitei and Kuki dominated areas.
GS2.trivia: Manipur, meaning 'A jeweled land', is known for its classical dance form, Manipuri, and is considered the birthplace of modern polo.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Delivers Landmark Verdict on Electoral Bonds Scheme
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a significant ruling, declared the Electoral Bonds Scheme unconstitutional, citing violations of citizens' right to information and transparency in political funding.
• The court directed the State Bank of India (SBI) to immediately cease issuing electoral bonds and to disclose all details of bonds purchased since 2019 to the Election Commission of India (ECI).
• The verdict emphasized that anonymous political donations undermine the principles of free and fair elections and democratic accountability.
• This decision is expected to have far-reaching implications for political parties and their funding mechanisms.
• The ECI has been tasked with publishing the disclosed information on its official website within a stipulated timeframe.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution - historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions
Parliament and State Legislatures – structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
prelimsPoints: The Electoral Bonds Scheme was introduced in 2017.
Article 19(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution guarantees the right to freedom of speech and expression, which includes the right to information.
The Election Commission of India (ECI) is a constitutional body responsible for conducting elections.
State Bank of India (SBI) was the only authorized bank to issue electoral bonds.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional implications of the Supreme Court's verdict on the Electoral Bonds Scheme.
Discuss the arguments for and against electoral bonds in promoting transparency and accountability in political funding.
Evaluate the potential impact of this judgment on political parties' fundraising strategies and electoral reforms in India.
Suggest alternative mechanisms for transparent political funding that uphold democratic principles.
GS2.trivia: The first electoral bonds were issued in March 2018.

GS2.headline: India, EU Announce Significant Progress in Free Trade Agreement Negotiations
GS2.explainer:
• India and the European Union (EU) concluded the latest round of Free Trade Agreement (FTA) negotiations, announcing significant progress on several key chapters.
• Both sides expressed optimism about reaching a comprehensive agreement, potentially by year-end, which could boost bilateral trade and investment.
• Discussions focused on market access for goods and services, intellectual property rights, and sustainable development provisions.
• The FTA aims to reduce tariffs, eliminate non-tariff barriers, and create a more predictable and transparent trade environment.
• A successful deal would be crucial for India's ambition to expand its global trade footprint and for the EU to diversify its supply chains.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora- their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: The European Union is an economic and political union of 27 member states.
An FTA aims to liberalize trade by reducing or eliminating tariffs and other trade barriers.
India is negotiating FTAs with several key partners, including the UK, Australia, and Canada.
The Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT) is responsible for implementing India's foreign trade policy.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic and economic significance of an India-EU Free Trade Agreement for both parties.
Discuss the potential challenges and opportunities for Indian industries arising from a comprehensive FTA with the EU.
Evaluate the role of FTAs in enhancing India's global trade competitiveness and achieving its economic goals.
Examine how sustainable development and labor standards are integrated into modern trade agreements.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and the European Union.
GS2.trivia: The EU is India's third-largest trading partner, after the US and China.

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Stricter Enforcement of Digital Personal Data Protection Act
GS2.explainer:
• A Parliamentary Standing Committee on Information Technology submitted its report, recommending stricter enforcement mechanisms for the recently enacted Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
• The committee highlighted concerns regarding the adequacy of the Data Protection Board's powers and resources to handle potential violations effectively.
• It suggested enhancing penalties for data breaches and ensuring swift grievance redressal for citizens.
• The report also called for greater public awareness campaigns about data protection rights and responsibilities.
• The recommendations aim to strengthen India's data governance framework and build trust in the digital economy.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
Role of civil services in a democracy.
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, aims to regulate the processing of digital personal data.
The Data Protection Board of India is established under this Act.
A Parliamentary Standing Committee is a permanent committee of Parliament.
The right to privacy was declared a fundamental right by the Supreme Court in the Puttaswamy judgment (2017).
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, and its significance for individual privacy.
Discuss the challenges in enforcing data protection laws in a rapidly evolving digital landscape.
Evaluate the recommendations of the Parliamentary Committee in strengthening the data governance framework.
Examine the balance between data innovation and individual privacy rights in India's digital economy.
GS2.imageDescription: A stylized image of data security and privacy.
GS2.trivia: India's first attempt at a comprehensive data protection law began with the Justice Srikrishna Committee report in 2018.

GS2.headline: New 'PM-Awas Grameen' Plus Scheme to Address Rural Housing Deficit
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved the launch of the 'Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana – Gramin (PMAY-G) Plus' scheme, a revamped version of the existing rural housing program.
• The 'Plus' scheme aims to achieve the 'Housing for All' target by 2027, focusing on providing pucca houses with basic amenities to eligible rural households.
• Enhanced financial assistance, convergence with other government schemes (like Swachh Bharat Mission for toilets), and use of innovative construction technologies are key features.
• Special emphasis will be placed on remote areas, tribal communities, and vulnerable groups to ensure inclusive development.
• The scheme is expected to generate significant employment in rural areas and improve the living standards of millions.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: PMAY-G was launched in 2016.
The scheme aims to provide a pucca house with basic amenities.
It operates under the Ministry of Rural Development.
Convergence with other schemes like MGNREGA (for labor) and Swachh Bharat Mission (for toilets) is a key feature.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and key features of the PMAY-G Plus scheme in addressing the rural housing deficit.
Discuss the challenges in implementing large-scale housing schemes in rural India, including land availability and material costs.
Evaluate the role of inter-scheme convergence in enhancing the effectiveness and impact of welfare programs.
Examine how improved housing contributes to socio-economic development and human dignity in rural communities.
GS2.imageDescription: A newly constructed rural house under a government scheme.
GS2.trivia: Before PMAY-G, the major rural housing program was the Indira Awaas Yojana (IAY).

GS2.headline: India Kicks Off Preparations for 5th India-Africa Forum Summit (IAFS)
GS2.explainer:
• India has initiated preparatory meetings for the 5th edition of the India-Africa Forum Summit (IAFS), expected to be held later this year.
• The summit aims to strengthen India's strategic partnership with African nations across various sectors, including trade, investment, capacity building, and defense.
• Discussions focused on setting the agenda, identifying key themes such as climate change, digital transformation, and health security, and ensuring wide participation.
• IAFS is India's premier mechanism for engaging with the entire African continent at a political level.
• This renewed engagement underscores India's commitment to South-South cooperation and its growing geopolitical significance in the Indo-Pacific.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora- their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: The first IAFS was held in 2008.
IAFS is a major platform for India's engagement with African countries.
India's 'Act East' policy has a strong economic and strategic dimension.
Africa is a continent of 54 recognized countries.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolution and significance of India's engagement with Africa, particularly through the IAFS framework.
Discuss the key areas of cooperation between India and African nations and the mutual benefits derived from this partnership.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India in strengthening its strategic and economic ties with the African continent.
Examine how India's approach to Africa differs from that of other major global powers and its implications for South-South cooperation.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and various African nations.
GS2.trivia: The 3rd IAFS, held in 2015, saw participation from all 54 African countries, making it the largest gathering of African leaders in India.

GS2.headline: Ministry of Health Launches Nationwide Campaign for NCD Prevention
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Ministry of Health and Family Welfare launched a comprehensive nationwide campaign aimed at preventing and controlling Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs).
• The campaign focuses on promoting healthy lifestyles, early detection, and management of NCDs such as diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular diseases, and cancers.
• Initiatives include widespread awareness programs, free screening camps, and improved access to diagnostics and treatment facilities at primary healthcare centers.
• Emphasis is also placed on reducing risk factors like tobacco use, unhealthy diets, physical inactivity, and harmful alcohol consumption.
• The move is crucial as NCDs account for a significant portion of mortality and morbidity in India, posing a major public health challenge.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: NCDs are diseases that are not transmitted from person to person.
Examples of NCDs include diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular diseases, and various cancers.
Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY) is a flagship health scheme.
The National Programme for Prevention & Control of Cancer, Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases & Stroke (NPCDCS) is a key initiative.
mainsPoints: Analyze the growing burden of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) in India and their socio-economic implications.
Discuss the multi-faceted approach required for effective NCD prevention and control, encompassing lifestyle changes, screening, and treatment.
Evaluate the role of government initiatives and public-private partnerships in strengthening India's public healthcare system to tackle NCDs.
Examine the challenges in promoting healthy behaviors and ensuring equitable access to NCD care across diverse populations.
GS2.imageDescription: People engaging in healthy activities like yoga and eating fruits.
GS2.trivia: Globally, NCDs kill 41 million people each year, equivalent to 74% of all deaths.

GS3.headline: Major Anti-Naxal Operation in Bijapur: 31 Naxalites, 2 Security Personnel Killed
GS3.explainer:
• A fierce encounter in Chhattisgarh's Bijapur district resulted in the death of 31 Naxalites and two security personnel.
• The joint operation involved forces from the District Reserve Guard (DRG), Special Task Force (STF), and CoBRA unit.
• This marks one of the deadliest clashes in recent years against Left-Wing Extremism (LWE) in the Bastar region.
• Security forces recovered a large cache of arms, ammunition, and Naxalite literature from the encounter site.
• The incident underscores the persistent challenge of LWE in central India and the ongoing efforts to neutralize the threat.
gsSubTopics: Linkages between development and spread of extremism
Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security

Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: Bijapur district is part of the Bastar region in Chhattisgarh, a known LWE stronghold.
DRG (District Reserve Guard), STF (Special Task Force), and CoBRA (Commando Battalion for Resolute Action) are specialized anti-Naxal units.
Left-Wing Extremism is primarily concentrated in the 'Red Corridor' region of India.
The term 'Naxalite' originated from Naxalbari village in West Bengal.
mainsPoints: Analyze the effectiveness of current government strategies in combating Left-Wing Extremism.
Discuss the socio-economic factors that contribute to the persistence of Naxalism in regions like Bastar.
Evaluate the challenges faced by security forces in anti-Naxal operations and suggest measures for improvement.
Examine the balance between security operations and developmental initiatives in addressing LWE.
GS3.imageDescription: Security personnel conducting an operation in a forest area.
GS3.trivia: The 'Red Corridor' is a region in eastern, central, and southern India that experiences considerable Naxalite-Maoist insurgency.

GS3.headline: IAF Mirage 2000 Fighter Jet Crashes in MP, Pilots Eject Safely
GS3.explainer:
• A Dassault Mirage 2000 fighter aircraft of the Indian Air Force (IAF) crashed during a routine training sortie in Shivpuri district, Madhya Pradesh.
• Both pilots on board managed to eject safely and were subsequently rescued with minor injuries.
• An inquiry has been ordered by the IAF to ascertain the cause of the accident.
• The Mirage 2000 is a multirole, single-engine fourth-generation fighter jet, a significant component of the IAF's fleet.
• The incident highlights the inherent risks associated with military aviation and the importance of stringent safety protocols and maintenance.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: Mirage 2000 is a French-made multirole fighter jet, manufactured by Dassault Aviation.
Shivpuri district is located in the Gwalior division of Madhya Pradesh.
The Indian Air Force is one of the three branches of the Indian Armed Forces.
Ejection seats are safety devices designed to rescue pilots in emergencies.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges in maintaining an aging fleet of fighter aircraft and the need for modernization in the IAF.
Examine the role of indigenous defence production in enhancing India's aerospace safety and self-reliance.
Analyze the factors contributing to military aircraft accidents and suggest measures for improving aviation safety.
Evaluate the importance of continuous training and advanced technology for pilot safety in high-performance aircraft.
GS3.imageDescription: A Dassault Mirage 2000 fighter jet in flight.
GS3.trivia: The Mirage 2000 was first inducted into the Indian Air Force in 1985 and played a crucial role in the Kargil War.

GS3.headline: RBI Holds Repo Rate Steady, Cites Inflation Concerns in MPC Meet
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) unanimously decided to maintain the repo rate at its current level for the sixth consecutive time.
• RBI Governor Shaktikanta Das highlighted persistent concerns over inflation, especially food inflation, as the primary reason for the pause.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target while supporting economic growth.
• The central bank revised its inflation projection marginally upwards for the next quarter, signaling a cautious outlook.
• Analysts anticipate that the RBI will continue to adopt a 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance, keeping liquidity tight.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.

Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System- objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
Food processing and related industries in India- scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management.
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) consists of six members (three from RBI, three external).
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
RBI's inflation target is 4% with a +/- 2% band.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors influencing the RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate amidst varying economic indicators.
Discuss the impact of a sustained 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance on different sectors of the Indian economy.
Evaluate the challenges posed by food inflation to India's overall inflation targeting framework.
Examine the role of the RBI's monetary policy in achieving macroeconomic stability and fostering sustainable growth.
GS3.imageDescription: Reserve Bank of India building in Mumbai.
GS3.trivia: The first Monetary Policy Committee meeting was held in October 2016.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite EOS-08
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched the Earth Observation Satellite (EOS-08) aboard the Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV-C58) from Sriharikota.
• EOS-08 is designed to provide high-resolution imagery for applications in agriculture, forestry, disaster management, and urban planning.
• This mission further strengthens India's remote sensing capabilities and its commitment to utilizing space technology for national development.
• The PSLV-C58 also carried several co-passenger satellites, including some from private Indian companies and international clients, promoting space collaboration.
• The launch demonstrates ISRO's continued prowess in deploying advanced satellites and its role in fostering India's growing space economy.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation) is India's national space agency.
PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) is a third-generation launch vehicle.
Earth observation satellites are used for monitoring Earth's surface, atmosphere, and oceans.
Sriharikota is the location of the Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC), India's primary spaceport.
mainsPoints: Discuss the applications of earth observation satellites in various sectors of the Indian economy and governance.
Evaluate India's achievements in space technology and its contributions to global space exploration.
Examine the role of ISRO in promoting private sector participation and fostering a vibrant space ecosystem in India.
Analyze the strategic importance of advanced remote sensing capabilities for national security and disaster management.
GS3.imageDescription: PSLV rocket lifting off from the launch pad.
GS3.trivia: PSLV holds the record for launching the most satellites in a single mission (104 satellites in 2017).

GS3.headline: Government Unveils National AI Strategy 2.0: Focus on Ethical AI & Skilling
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched the National AI Strategy 2.0, building on the previous 'AI for All' vision.
• The updated strategy emphasizes responsible and ethical development of Artificial Intelligence, with a focus on data privacy and algorithmic transparency.
• Key pillars include fostering AI innovation in critical sectors like healthcare and agriculture, promoting AI skilling, and establishing a robust governance framework.
• The strategy also outlines plans for setting up dedicated AI research centers and encouraging public-private partnerships in AI development.
• India aims to position itself as a global leader in ethical and inclusive AI, leveraging its demographic dividend and technological prowess.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: MeitY (Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology) is the nodal ministry for AI policy in India.
The first National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence was released in 2018 by NITI Aayog.
Ethical AI concerns include bias, privacy, and accountability.
India is a member of the Global Partnership on Artificial Intelligence (GPAI).
mainsPoints: Discuss the key objectives and components of India's National AI Strategy 2.0 and its potential impact on various sectors.
Analyze the ethical challenges associated with the rapid development and deployment of Artificial Intelligence.
Evaluate India's potential to become a global hub for ethical and inclusive AI, considering its strengths and weaknesses.
Examine the role of government policy in fostering AI innovation while addressing concerns related to job displacement and data security.
GS3.trivia: India is one of the founding members of the Global Partnership on Artificial Intelligence (GPAI).

GS3.headline: Defence Ministry Greenlights Indigenous Submarine Development Project
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Defence announced the approval of a mega 'Make in India' project for the indigenous design and construction of advanced conventional submarines.
• This strategic move aims to bolster India's naval capabilities and achieve greater self-reliance in critical defence technologies.
• The project, under 'Strategic Partnership' model, will involve collaboration between an Indian private sector shipyard and a foreign original equipment manufacturer (OEM).
• The new submarines are expected to feature advanced stealth, combat management systems, and long-range targeting capabilities.
• This initiative is a significant step towards fulfilling the Indian Navy's long-term modernization plans and strengthening maritime security.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: The 'Make in India' initiative promotes domestic manufacturing.
The 'Strategic Partnership' model aims to create a robust defence industrial base.
Conventional submarines are diesel-electric submarines, unlike nuclear submarines.
The Indian Navy is one of the three branches of the Indian Armed Forces.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic rationale behind India's push for indigenous defence production, particularly in critical areas like submarine development.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities associated with the 'Strategic Partnership' model in fostering self-reliance in defence manufacturing.
Evaluate the importance of a strong naval force, including advanced submarines, for India's maritime security and geopolitical interests.
Examine the technological advancements and skill development required to achieve sustained indigenous capability in complex defence systems.
GS3.imageDescription: A silhouette of a submarine at sea.
GS3.trivia: Project 75 (I) is a previous Indian Navy project for the acquisition of six conventional submarines.

GS3.headline: Report Highlights Impact of Climate Change on Indian Agriculture
GS3.explainer:
• A comprehensive report by the Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) detailed the severe and varied impacts of climate change on Indian agriculture.
• The report points to increased frequency of extreme weather events, such as droughts, floods, and unseasonal rains, leading to significant crop losses.
• It also highlights shifts in cropping patterns, reduced yield potential of major crops, and increased vulnerability to pests and diseases due to changing climatic conditions.
• The findings emphasize the urgent need for climate-resilient agriculture practices, improved irrigation systems, and development of stress-tolerant crop varieties.
• Food security and farmers' livelihoods are at significant risk, necessitating immediate policy interventions and technological advancements.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.

Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System- objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: ICAR (Indian Council of Agricultural Research) is an autonomous organization under the Department of Agricultural Research and Education (DARE), Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare.
India is highly vulnerable to climate change due to its large agricultural sector and monsoon dependence.
M.S. Swaminathan is known as the 'Father of the Green Revolution' in India.
Droughts, floods, and heatwaves are common extreme weather events in India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multifaceted impacts of climate change on Indian agriculture and its implications for food security and rural livelihoods.
Discuss the various strategies and technologies available for building climate resilience in the agricultural sector.
Evaluate the role of government policies, research institutions, and farmer-centric initiatives in adapting to climate change.
Examine the challenges in implementing climate-resilient agriculture practices across diverse agro-climatic zones in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A field of crops affected by drought.
GS3.trivia: Agriculture contributes around 18-20% of India's GDP and employs over 50% of the workforce.
